Title: Plant diterpene synthases: exploring modularity and metabolic diversity for bioengineering

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Downregulation of ERG9 increases flux toward isoprenyl diphosphate intermediates and supports improved diterpenoid production.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ERG9 is discussed as an endogenous competing-pathway gene in yeast diterpenoid engineering. 2. Downregulation of ERG9 was used to increase precursor availability for terpenoid production. 3. Its role is to reduce carbon loss into sterol/squalene biosynthesis rather than directly catalyze diterpenoid scaffold formation.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Possible disruption of native sterol metabolism and host fitness if excessively repressed.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful host-engineering target for improving microbial diterpenoid production when combined with precursor-supply enhancement and balanced pathway expression.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This is a review paper; ERG9 is summarized as a metabolic engineering lever, not as the main biological subject of a single primary experiment in this article.

Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: GGPP; in some cases NNPP or class II diTPS products such as CPP, LPP, PPP
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: diverse diterpene hydrocarbon and oxygenated scaffolds
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyze the first committed cyclization and/or rearrangement reactions of diterpenoid biosynthesis, converting GGPP or prenyl diphosphate intermediates into diverse diterpene scaffolds.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: directly forms scaffold precursor of target diterpenoids
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Established as the first committed and central diversification step of diterpenoid metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Determine diterpenoid scaffold identity and thereby control metabolic diversity and engineering potential.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DiTPS enzymes form the core committed step of plant diterpenoid biosynthesis. 2. Structural and pathway modularity of diTPSs underlies massive diterpenoid chemical diversity. 3. Combinatorial use of class II, class I, and bifunctional diTPSs enables natural and engineered production of valuable diterpenoids.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical enzyme characterization; heterologous expression; phylogeny-guided discovery; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: TPS family; class I, class II, or bifunctional class I/II diterpene synthases with alpha, beta, gamma domains
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This review centers broadly on the diTPS family rather than one single gene.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Major enzyme toolkit for synthetic biology, pathway reconstruction, and combinatorial diterpenoid bioengineering.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Pacific yew; microbial and plant heterologous systems
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cyclizes GGPP to taxadiene, the central diterpene intermediate/precursor in taxol biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms immediate core precursor for paclitaxel biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a central pathway entry enzyme for taxol precursor formation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enables production of taxadiene for paclitaxel pathway engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TXS is highlighted as a Taxus-specific class I diTPS. 2. It forms taxadiene, the central intermediate for the anti-cancer agent taxol. 3. TXS has been used in microbial and plant engineering platforms for pharmaceutical precursor production.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High-value target for paclitaxel precursor production in engineered microbes and plants.

Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: GGPP or (+)-CPP depending on enzyme context
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: levopimaradiene; abietadiene; 13-hydroxy-8(14)-abietene epimers as initial products in some LAS enzymes
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes formation of abietane/pimarane-related diterpene scaffolds, including levopimaradiene/abietadiene-type products in conifer oleoresin metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms hydrocarbon or alcohol scaffold precursors of diterpene resin acids
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes formation of conifer defensive diterpene scaffold precursors used for DRA biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Conifer LAS enzymes are central scaffold-forming diTPSs in oleoresin diterpenoid metabolism. 2. Norway spruce LAS was shown to form diterpenol epimers that dehydrate to olefin products. 3. LAS functions have been confirmed across conifers and exploited for engineering DRAs and related compounds.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical enzyme assays; comparative functional characterization; heterologous expression
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense against insect pests and pathogens; industrial feedstock for coatings and inks.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for engineering resin-acid-related diterpenoids and redirecting conifer terpenoid capacity toward valuable products.
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: bifunctional or monofunctional diterpene synthase depending species context

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts the cisoid isomer of GGPP, nerylneryl diphosphate, into lycosantalene.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enables lycosantalene formation from NNPP, expanding known substrate space for plant diTPSs.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Tomato TPS21 was identified as the first diTPS reported to use NNPP. 2. It converts NNPP into lycosantalene. 3. This discovery broadened understanding of diTPS substrate and product diversity.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Direct class I conversion of GGPP into the belowground defense diterpene rhizathalene.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Produces the belowground defense metabolite rhizathalene.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Arabidopsis rhizathalene synthase was identified as a new class I diTPS. 2. It directly forms rhizathalene from GGPP. 3. Rhizathalene functions as a belowground defense metabolite.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical characterization; gene discovery linked to metabolite function

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: plants; specifically tomato in one engineering example
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes the first step of the MEP pathway, increasing plastidial isoprenoid precursor supply for GGPP and downstream diterpenoids.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Described as a key enzyme of the MEP pathway and overexpressed to improve precursor supply.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enhances precursor availability and improves engineered diterpenoid production.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DXS is identified as a key MEP-pathway enzyme. 2. Overexpression of rate-limiting MEP/MVA genes, including DXS, increased isoprenyl diphosphate formation in terpenoid production systems. 3. Coexpression of tomato DXS improved diterpenoid engineering in N. benthamiana.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important upstream engineering target to increase flux into plastid-derived diterpenoids.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: plants; specifically tobacco in one example
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Forms geranylgeranyl diphosphate, the general precursor for diterpenoid biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: GGPP formation is described as challenging and important for diterpenoid engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Improved GGPP supply supports increased diterpenoid production.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GGPP synthase produces the common diterpenoid precursor GGPP. 2. Limited GGPP supply is a major bottleneck in engineered diterpenoid production. 3. Coexpression of a tobacco GGPP synthase improved diterpenoid engineering in N. benthamiana.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Priority precursor-supply target in diterpenoid engineering.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Native product specificity was redirected toward GGPP formation to improve diterpenoid precursor supply in yeast.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Redirecting product specificity improved GGPP supply in yeast diterpenoid production systems.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Yeast farnesyl diphosphate synthase was engineered to redirect product specificity toward GGPP. 2. This improved precursor supply for diterpenoid production. 3. It illustrates precursor-pathway rewiring as a core strategy in metabolic engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Protein engineering; metabolic engineering in yeast
